title: Day 24 — Mock Frontend Interview
summary: Final day: 10-minute reading, five timed mock interview rounds, self-evaluation rubric, graduation checklist, and congratulations — you complete the FrontForge React Interview Track today.

blocks.content: This is the final day of the FrontForge React Interview Track. No new learning. No new concepts. You will run five timed interview rounds back-to-back — exactly how a real frontend loop feels. JavaScript, React, machine coding, project discussion, debugging. Then you self-evaluate, check off your graduation list, and graduate.

blocks.title: Graduation day
blocks.content: You started with components and props. You built APIs, Context, Redux, RTK Query, auth, dashboards, and machine coding projects. Today you prove you can explain, build, debug, and optimize under interview pressure. After today, start applying.
Skim this lesson once. Set up your environment: quiet space, timer, water, official docs tabs ready. Close AI assistants and Google. Official documentation only — same rules as real interviews.

items.text: Timer app ready — you will run 30 + 30 + 60 + 45 + 30 minutes across five rounds
Tabs open: react.dev, MDN, React Router docs, Redux Toolkit docs — official only
FrontForge repo and your refactored Day 23 project ready for Round 4 discussion
Optional: record yourself — communication score matters
No AI. No Google. No Stack Overflow. Official docs only for Round 3.

blocks.title: Interviewer script
blocks.content: We will cover JavaScript fundamentals for 30 minutes. I will ask conceptual questions and may ask you to trace code or explain behavior. Talk through your thinking.

rows: Function remembers outer scope; hooks and debounce examples
Cannot give a concrete React example
Sugar over Promises; try/catch for errors
Forget fetch does not throw on 404
Delay until pause; search input pattern
Explicit binding; arrow functions inherit lexical this
Lose this in class method callback

blocks.title: JavaScript round strategy
blocks.content: Start with the definition, give one React example, stop. Interviewers interrupt long answers. If stuck, trace the code line by line out loud.

blocks.title: Interviewer script
blocks.content: React round — 30 minutes. Components through performance. I want concise answers with trade-offs, not textbook dumps.

blocks.title: React topics — confirm you can explain each

items.text: JSX — expressions in braces, one root, fragments
Components — functional, composition, single responsibility
Props — data down, callbacks up, read-only
State — useState local, lift when siblings share
useEffect — external sync, deps, cleanup
useMemo / useCallback — stable refs and expensive compute
useRef — DOM access, mutable value without re-render
Context — global low-frequency, memoize value
Redux — slices, actions, selectors, immutable updates
RTK Query — cache, invalidation, loading/error state
Routing — Route, Link, useParams, protected routes
Performance — profile first, memo where measured, lazy routes

blocks.content: Your assigned project today (randomly selected from the interview pool): see the challenge section. Time limit: 60 minutes. Requirements: responsive, TypeScript, API integration, clean architecture, loading, error, and empty states. No AI. No Google. Official documentation only.

items.text: 0–5 min: Clarify scope, pick MVP, define types
5–25 min: Static UI + local state working
25–45 min: API integration, loading, error, empty states
45–55 min: Responsive polish, shared components
55–60 min: Self-test, prepare 60-second architecture pitch

blocks.content: The interviewer opens your FrontForge repo and your Day 23 refactored project. You explain architecture, design decisions, challenges, and improvements — like a real system design discussion scoped to frontend.

FrontForge is a JSON-driven React learning platform. Curriculum lives in content/dayNN/*.json files validated by Zod schemas. A content engine loads and renders lessons dynamically — no hardcoded lesson pages per day.
Describe src/features, components (atoms/molecules), content-engine (schemas, repository, renderer), routes, and hooks. Explain separation: content data vs rendering logic vs app shell.

Lesson blocks are discriminated unions by type — heading, paragraph, code, checklist, table, accordion, challenge reference, etc. A block renderer maps type to React component. Adding a new block type means schema + component + registry entry.
Why JSON curriculum? Scalable content without redeploying UI. Why Zod? Runtime + compile-time safety on authored content. Why feature folders? Colocation as the track grew from 7 to 24 days.
Be honest: schema evolution, block type extensibility, validation in CI, keeping interview week content consistent with renderer capabilities. Interviewers respect struggle + resolution.
Progress persistence, spaced repetition on quiz wrong answers, E2E tests on renderer, CMS for non-developer authors, analytics on weak topics. Show product thinking.
Fix bugs in provided starter code or audit your Round 3 submission. Categories: React bugs, performance issues, TypeScript errors, routing problems. Document root cause in one sentence per bug.

rows: Infinite render, stale UI, wrong list item
Slow typing, janky scroll, hot parent re-renders
Blank page, 404 on refresh, wrong param

blocks.title: Debugging communication
blocks.content: Narrate your process: reproduce → isolate → hypothesize → fix → verify. Interviewers score the process, not just the fix.
Rate yourself honestly on each category from 1 to 5 stars. A 3 means interview-ready with gaps. A 5 means you would hire yourself today. Be honest — this drives your post-graduation study plan.

rows: Cannot explain closures or event loop
Traces async code confidently under pressure
Does not finish MVP in time
Ships core features in time box

blocks.title: Record your scores
blocks.content: Copy this rubric into your reflection. Categories below 3 get one week of focused reps after graduation.

items.text: React Fundamentals — components, props, state, effects, hooks
JavaScript Fundamentals — closures, async, arrays, event loop
TypeScript Basics — interfaces, types, API models, strict mode
Redux Toolkit — slices, selectors, immutable updates
RTK Query — caching, mutations, invalidation
Routing — React Router, params, protected routes
APIs — fetch, error handling, loading states
Performance — memo, lazy, code splitting, profiling
Debugging — infinite render, mutation, deps, keys, routing
Machine Coding — timed builds with clean architecture
Project Discussion — explain architecture and trade-offs
GitHub Portfolio Updated — best projects pinned with READMEs
Resume Updated — frontend projects and skills listed
LinkedIn Updated — headline reflects frontend engineer readiness

blocks.title: Congratulations
blocks.content: You have completed the FrontForge React Interview Track. You should now be ready to start applying for Frontend Engineer positions and continue improving through real interviews.
Interviews are another form of practice. Don't wait until you feel 100% ready. Start applying, learn from each interview, and improve continuously.
You built real projects. You refactored for production. You survived five mock rounds. The gap between you and the offer is reps — real interviews, real feedback, real improvement. Go apply.

items.text: Round 1 JavaScript — 30 minutes complete
Round 2 React — 30 minutes complete
Round 3 Machine Coding — 60 minutes complete
Round 4 Project Discussion — FrontForge + portfolio
Round 5 Debugging — all scenarios fixed
Self-evaluation rubric completed
Quiz passed
Final reflection submitted
